coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots anchoring MnO2/graphene aerogel exhibits excellent performance as electrode materials for supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Haipeng
Yuan, Yue
Xu, Qunjie
Liu, Haimei
Wang, Yong-Gang
Xia, Yongyao
coredata.dc:description: Manganese oxides/graphene composite material has become regarded as a highly promising electrode material for high-performance supercapacitors. However, some shortages such as low bonding strength and poor stability are faced to solve. In this work, carbon quantum dots serve as the bridge for connecting MnO2 and graphene, which contribute to synthesize the stable MnO2/carbon quantum dots/graphene composite aerogel. Due to the connection function of CQDs the stable combinations are formed between MnO2 nanoparticles and graphene nanosheets. Subsequently, the composite aerogel as-fabricated has the three-dimensional net structure, indicating a large specific surface area and abundant electron transport pathways. The MnO2/CQDs/GA electrode exhibits excellent electrochemical performance as compared to those of MnO2/GA and MnO2/G that are synthesized without the addition of CQDs. This composite electrode displays high specific capacitance of 721 F g-1 at 1 A g−1, good rate capability of 89.2% capacitance retention at 20 A g−1 and remarkable cycle stability of 92.3% capacitance retention after 10,000 at 10 A g−1. Moreover, the MnO2/CQDs/GA is quite stable and has preeminent electrochemical performance, which demonstrates the great potential for the development of high-performance supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
MnO2
Graphene aerogel
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration demonstrating the fabrication procedure of the MnO2/CQDs/graphene composite aerogel.
FESEM images of (a) MnO2/CQDs/GA, (b) MnO2/GA, (c) MnO2/G; (d) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms, (e) Pore size distributions, (f) Raman spectra of MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA, MnO2/G.
XRD patterns of MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA, MnO2/G.
HRTEM images of (a) CQDs/GA, (b) MnO2/CQDs/GA, (c) MnO2/GA, (d) MnO2/G.
(a) Comparison of XPS full survey scans of the samples. High-resolution XPS curves of O1s for (b) MnO2/CQDs/GA, (c) MnO2/GA, (d) MnO2/G.

(a) CV curves at 10 mV s-1, (b) GCD curves at 1 A g-1 of three samples; (c) CV curves and (d) GCD curves of MnO2/CQDs/GA; (e) rate performance, (f) cycling performances and coulombic efficiency of three samples for 10,000 charge−discharge cycles at 10 A g-1.

Electrochemical performance of MnO2/CQDs/GA//MnO2/CQDs/GA measured in a two electrode system. (a) CV curves at different scan rates from 5 to 200 mV s-1. (b) GCD curves at different current densities from 1 to 20 A g-1. (c) Cycling stability measured at a current density of 20 A g-1 for 10,000 cycles, inset showing the specific capacitances at different current densities from 1 to 20 A g-1. (d) Ragone plot related to energy densities and power densities.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots anchoring MnO2/graphene aerogel exhibits excellent performance as electrode materials for supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai, 200090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power
College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Molecular Catalysis and Innovative Materials, Institute of New Energy, Fudan University, Shanghai, 200433, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Molecular Catalysis and Innovative Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Manganese oxides/graphene composite material has become regarded as a highly promising electrode material for high-performance supercapacitors. However, some shortages such as low bonding strength and poor stability are faced to solve. In this work, carbon quantum dots serve as the bridge for connecting MnO2 and graphene, which contribute to synthesize the stable MnO2/carbon quantum dots/graphene composite aerogel. Due to the connection function of CQDs the stable combinations are formed between MnO2 nanoparticles and graphene nanosheets. Subsequently, the composite aerogel as-fabricated has the three-dimensional net structure, indicating a large specific surface area and abundant electron transport pathways. The MnO2/CQDs/GA electrode exhibits excellent electrochemical performance as compared to those of MnO2/GA and MnO2/G that are synthesized without the addition of CQDs. This composite electrode displays high specific capacitance of 721 F g-1 at 1 A g−1, good rate capability of 89.2% capacitance retention at 20 A g−1 and remarkable cycle stability of 92.3% capacitance retention after 10,000 at 10 A g−1. Moreover, the MnO2/CQDs/GA is quite stable and has preeminent electrochemical performance, which demonstrates the great potential for the development of high-performance supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors as one of the emerging energy storage devices have gradually become the research focus in recent years [1–4]. However, present supercapacitors are still unable to completely meet the growing demands for efficient energy storage, which means that it is urgent to design the supercapacitors with high energy density, power density and stability. Manganese oxides (MnO2) is a promising electrode material for high-performance supercacitors because of high theoretical specific capacitance (1370 F g−1), wide potential window, abundance and environmentally benign nature [5–8]. But some drawbacks of MnO2 such as poor electron conductivity and instability restrict the practical application for these supercapacitors. In order to overcome these shortcomings, enormous efforts have been made in the past decades. Multifarious crystal structure, morphology and composites have been fabricated to improve the electrochemical performance of MnO2 electrodes [9–13].

Among these designs of MnO2 electrode materials, carbon materials as the optimal material were used to composite with MnO2, which have been widely reported in the current study [14–19]. Because of the characteristics such as large specific surface area and high conductivity, carbon materials can improve the shortcomings of MnO2 effectively. In particular, graphene as a one-atom-thick two-dimensional carbon material has attracted intensive attention. It is widely applied in electrode materials of supercapacitors due to its high surface area, mechanical strength, electrical conductivity, stability and low density. In fact, compared to the pristine MnO2, it has already been clearly demonstrated that various MnO2 composites formed with graphene exhibit high specific capacitance and good cycle stability in previous investigations [20–27]. Yao et al. [28] successfully synthesized mesoporous MnO2 nanosphere/graphene composite displayed excellent electrochemical performance as compared to the pure MnO2 and graphene. The composite electrode exhibits superior specific capacitance of 466.7 F g−1, remarkable cycle stability of 92% capacitance retention after 2000 at 1 A g−1.

However, it is noticeable that graphene nanosheets are apt to reunite in aqueous solution, which results that the actual specific surface area is much less than the theoretical specific surface area. What's worse, it also leads to less active layer and higher internal resistance. In order to avoid the re-stacking of graphene nanosheets, the three-dimensional net structure graphene aerogel has been assembled. Porous structure of the graphene aerogel is conducive to enlarge the active areas of electrode materials contacted with electrolyte and reduce the internal resistance. Net structure can prevent the nanosheets aggregating effectively. Therefore, the three-dimensional net structure graphene aerogel as supporting material in composite can provide large surface area to load other materials, multiple channels to transport the electrolyte ions and interlaced conductive network [29–35]. However, the weak bonding strength between the rigid inorganic metal oxide, such as MnO2, and the graphene leads to less improvements of conductivity and stability. Hence, it is highly desirable to add a stable and firm link between MnO2 and graphene to fabricate a stable and high-performance MnO2/graphene composite material.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as one of the emerging carbon materials attract much attention because of their small size (below 10 nm), various functional groups and photoluminescence properties. CQDs can be well combined with inorganic and organic materials, which are contributed to the enhancing of bonding strength between multiple materials. Current studies indicated that CQDs can be used as the connection of the composite material that presents the excellent and stable electrochemical property [36–42]. Zhang et al. [43] used CQDs to link GO film and polypyrrole, which accelerated the electron transportation and increased the dielectric constant. The synthesized ternary composite exhibited high specific capacitance and cycle stability. All these studies show that CQDs as the link in the composite materials can not only improve the bonding strength among each component of the composites, but can also reduce the internal resistance and charge transfer resistance.

In this work, we synthesized the MnO2/carbon quantum dots/graphene composite aerogel (MnO2/CQDs/GA) by employing CQDs to connect MnO2 and graphene. Compared to MnO2/graphene composite aerogel (MnO2/GA) and MnO2/graphene (MnO2/G) synthesized in the same conditions just without CQDs, the MnO2/CQDs/GA exhibited outstanding electrochemical performance, which indicated that CQDs and the three-dimensional net structure can efficiently improve the conductivity and stability of the composite. The formed three-dimensional net structure can enlarged the contact area with electrolyte and increased the ion diffusion channels. In addition, the added CQDs not only contribute to enhance the bonding strength between MnO2 and graphene, but also improve the specific capacitance and cycle stability. These advantages can lead to the significant improvement of the capacitive performances of the MnO2/CQDs/GA that are constructed with CQDs and 3D net structure.

The citric acid was used as carbon source to prepare the CQDs by a hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 h, which has been reported in our previous work [44]. The obtained CQDs were well dispersed in 40 mL deionised water under sonication, which the concentration was 125 μg mL−1.

The CQDs/GA was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from conductive squama graphite by the modified Hummers' method [45]. Secondly, the homogenous GO aqueous dispersion was formed by dispersing the as-synthesized GO in the as-prepared CQDs solution with ultrasonication for 2 h. Thirdly, the dispersion was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. The hydrothermal reaction products were centrifuged and washed with deionised water for several times. Finally, the CQDs/GA was obtained by freeze-drying for 24 h.

The obtained CQDs/GA were cut into some pieces and added into the deionised water and fully dispersed with stirring. Subsequently, the dispersion was mixed with 5 mM KMnO4 and heated at 80 °C for 4 h. After the reaction finished, the as-synthesized MnO2/CQDs/GA was collected via vacuum filtration, washed with deionised water, and freeze-dried. In order to investigate the significant roles played by the CQDs in the composite material, the MnO2/GA without CQDs was synthesized with the same conditions and similar procedures. Besides, to investigate the effect of the aerogel structure, the MnO2/G was prepared by using the same amount of graphene reacted with KMnO4. The graphene was reduced by hydrazine hydrate without the three-dimensional aerogel net structure.

The X-ray diffraction patterns of the as-prepared MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G were collected by utilizing a Bruker D8 Advance spectrometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss Supra 55) was used to characterize the morphology of the samples. To further investigate the microstructure, the samples were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The specific surface area and the pore size distribution were calculated by using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, respectively. To investigate the defects of different samples, a LabRAM HR furnished with 532 nm laser was employed to record Raman spectra.

The MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G were used to fabricate the working electrodes. The three samples were pressed to form films by the roller machine, and then the square (1 × 1 cm2) cut from the films was directly pressed on the pre-treated nickel foam. The working electrodes were obtained after drying at 60 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven. The active material loadings were determined by weighing the Ni foam substrate before and after applying the active material, which calculated that about 1.0–2.0 mg of the active materials on the Ni foam was loaded.

A three-electrode system was fabricated with the as-prepared working electrode, counter electrode (Pt foil) and reference electrode (Ag/AgCl). The electrochemistry tests including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic current charge-discharge (GCD) were measured in 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution.

The symmetrical supercapacitor device was assembled with the two as-prepared electrodes that have the same quality MnO2/CQDs/GA (about 1 mg). The MnO2/CQDs/GA//MnO2/CQDs/GA symmetrical capacitor was also tested by CV and GCD in a two-electrode system to characterize electrochemical properties. The electrochemistry tests were also measured in 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. All of the electrochemical measurements in three-electrode system and two-electrode system were implemented by using Solartron 1470 E electrochemical workstation.

The specific capacitance (Cs, F g−1) of the MnO2/CQDs/GA in the three-electrode system was calculated according to eq (1) [46]:
(1) Cs= (I × Δ t) / (m × ΔV)
Where I is the discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), m is the mass of the active material (g), and ΔV is the discharge potential window (V).

The specific capacitance (Csp, F g−1), the energy density (Esp, Wh kg−1) and the power density (Psp, W kg−1) of the MnO2/CQDs/GA//MnO2/CQDs/GA symmetrical capacitor were calculated according to the following eqs (2)–(4):
(2) Csp = (I × Δt) / (M × ΔV)
(3) Esp = 0.5 × Csp × ΔV2 /3.6
(4) Psp = Esp / Δt
Where M is the mass of both electrode materials (g), and ΔV is the discharge voltage window (V). Δt is the discharge time (s).

As shown in Fig. 1 , it is the synthesis pathway of the MnO2/CQDs/GA. The 3D CQDs/graphene composite aerogels formed by hydrothermal method are used as the reducing agent, which react with the certain concentration KMnO4 to synthesize the MnO2 nanoparticles on the graphene nanosheets. The reaction mechanism of the redox reaction between graphene and KMnO4 is based on following reaction formula as reported [47–49]. (3C + 4KMnO4 + H2O → 4MnO2 + K2CO3 + 2KHCO3). It indicated that the MnO2 content in MnO2/CQDs/GA could be estimated and controlled by adjusting the concentration of KMnO4. Simultaneously, because of numerous functional groups contained on the CQDs, such as hydroxyl, and C=O, C-O and C-H bonds in Fig. S1, a number of links are formed between graphene nanosheets and MnO2 nanoparticles, which improve the bonding strength among the components of the MnO2/CQDs/GA.

In order to describe the morphologies of the composites, the three obtained samples (MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G) were characterized by FESEM as shown in Fig. 2 . The FESEM images of three samples show that the MnO2 nanoparticles (below 50 nm) formed and distributed on the surface of the graphene, which are consistent with the expected results. In sharp contrast to the agglomerated structure shown in Fig. 2(c) that do not have a network structure (MnO2/G), the three-dimensional network structures are built in Fig. 2(a and b), which display large contact areas of graphene nanosheets supporting the irregular MnO2 nanoparticles, the interconnected conductive network, loading more MnO2 nanoparticles, and prevent the agglomeration of graphene effectively. These advantages promote the ion transport in electrode/electrolyte and active-site accessibility. It can also be clearly observed that different from the well distribution of nanoparticles in Fig. 2(a and b), the MnO2 nanoparticles on the graphene occur with serious agglomeration in Fig. 2(c). Meanwhile, the effect of the MnO2 content in the MnO2/CQDs/GA was explored with the concentration of KMnO4 (6 mM, 7 mM, 8 mM). As shown in Fig. S2, the stability of the aerogel structure and the conductivity of the MnO2/CQDs/GA tend to reduce with the MnO2 content increases, which indicated that the optimal MnO2 content is 50%. In addition, it can also be exemplified by the element mapping images of the MnO2/CQDs/GA to illustrate the element distribution in Fig. S3. It is found that the distributions of C, O, Mn are consistent with the select area of the sample, which indicated that the MnO2 nanoparticles are well distributed on the dimensional reticulated graphene.

To clarify the superiority of the 3D network structure of aerogel further, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas and the pore size distribution were characterized as shown in Fig. 2(d), and the specific surface areas of the MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G are calculated to reach 144.5, 117.3, 49.2 m2 g−1, respectively. It is quite evident that the MnO2/CQDs/GA creates the largest active specific surfaces, contributing to the electrolyte soaking into the electrode. Moreover, the pore size distributions were measured by the BJH analysis in Fig. 2(e), exhibiting that the MnO2/CQDs/GA has the porous structure with the pore diameters mainly between 3 and 10 nm. The porous structure of the MnO2/CQDs/GA not only provides the more channels for ion-diffusion, but also enlarges the areas of the MnO2/CQDs/GA soaked with the electrolyte.

Raman spectroscopy as an effective way to characterize the order and disorder of carbon-based composite materials was used to illustrate the internal defects of the obtained samples in Fig. 2(f). The two obviously peaks of the MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G appeared at 1350 cm−1 (D band) and 1590 cm−1 (G band), which were in the same positions as the peaks of the graphene aerogel. The D band was refers to a disordered graphitic lattice-graphene layer edge, while the G band can be ascribed to the ideal graphitic lattice. The Raman spectra suggested that the GO in the three samples were well reduced. The peak intensity ratios (ID/IG) of the MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G are calculated to be 1.17, 1.14, and 1.06, respectively, which correspond well to the results of the BET surface areas above. It is clearly indicated again that there are the most defects in the MnO2/CQDs/GA, which are helpful to the improvement of the electrochemical properties because of the convenient ion-transport. In addition, there is a peak appeared at 635 cm−1 in the three samples assigned to the Mn-O stretching vibration [50], demonstrating the formation of the MnO2.

As already reported, α-MnO2 has a superior specific capacitance because of its large tunnel size [51–53]. Therefore, to confirm the crystal structures of the MnO2 in the synthesized composite materials, the XRD analysis was implemented and shown in Fig. 3 . The diffraction peaks of the synthesized MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G appeared in accordance with the α-MnO2 (JCPDS: 44–0141). This indicated that all the nanoparticles formed on the graphene surface are the α-MnO2. And the peak at approximately 25°corresponds to the (002) reflection of graphene, which shows that the nanosheets in the three composite materials are well-reduced graphene. These diffraction peaks of the three samples exhibit that the α-MnO2 nanoparticles are combined with the graphene nanosheets in the three composite materials.

HRTEM was applied to further investigate the microstructures and the compositions of the three samples. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the HRTEM image shows that the CQDs (under 4 nm) with the lattice spacing of 0.34 nm are homogeneously distributed on the graphene surface. Moreover, we can observe that the MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G were mainly fabricated by graphene nanosheets/MnO2 nanoparticles in Fig. 4(b, c, d). In particularly, the CQDs as a component of the MnO2/CQDs/GA composite material can be found in Fig. 4(b). In addition, the lattice spacing of 0.21, 0.27 nm shown in Fig. 4(b, c, d) correspond to the (310), (411) crystal planes of the α-MnO2. Due to the clearly visible lattice fringes, it is demonstrated that the MnO2, CQDs, graphene of the three samples have the high crystallinity.

To reveal the effect of the CQDs in the MnO2/CQDs/GA, the three samples were characterized by XPS. The full XPS spectra display that the component elements of the MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G are C, O, Mn in Fig. 5 (a). And as shown in Fig. 5(b, c, d), the O1s XPS spectra of all the three samples can be divided into four parts, which are correspondences to the Mn-O (529.6 eV), Mn-O-C (530 eV), Mn-OH/C=O (531.5 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (533 eV) groups [54–56]. Compared to the Mn-O-C bonding in MnO2/GA and MnO2/G, the intensity of the bonding peak is much larger in Fig. 5(b). It is indicated that the bonding strength between the MnO2 and graphene have been increased with the addition of the CQDs. The CQDs play the role like rivet to reinforce the connection among the components of the MnO2/CQDs/GA, which contribute to promote the stability and electro-conductibility. Besides, by contrasting the C1s curves of three samples in Fig. S4 (a-c), the intensity of O=C-O, C-O bonds in the MnO2/CQDs/GA is obviously increased, which also indicated that the link between MnO2 and graphene become firm. In addition, by contrasting the Mn2p curves of three samples in Fig. S4 (d-f), it can be seen that the element Mn of as-prepared samples mainly exist in the chemical valence states of Mn (IV).

As the characterization analyses on morphology and structure above, the composite aerogel that has the CQDs sandwiched between the MnO2 nanoparticles and the three-dimensional graphene aerogel has been synthesized. The formation of the 3D network structure is conducive to improve the electrochemical properties of the MnO2/CQDs/GA, because it can enlarge the areas of the active materials contacted with the electrolyte and provide numerous channels to transport electrons. Moreover, the CQDs existing in the composite aerogel also play the important role in the stability and conductivity improvements, which can enhance the relationships between the components of the composite aerogel and reduce the internal resistance of the composite materials.

The excellent electrochemical performance of the MnO2/CQDs/GA was revealed by the CV and GCD tests. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), all the CV curves of the MnO2/CQDs/GA, MnO2/GA and MnO2/G scanned at10 mV s−1 show rectangular shape. By comparison, it is indicated that the composite with the 3D structure have the higher specific capacity. But beyond that, the addition of the CQDs can also increase the specific capacity. The same conclusions are obtained by comparing the GCD curves of the three samples at current densities of 1 A g−1 in Fig. 6(b). According to eq (1), the specific capacities of the three samples are calculated to be 721, 527, 354 F g−1, respectively. Due to the aerogel structure, more active materials, especially the MnO2 nanoparticles, contacted with the electrolyte for alkali cations intercalation/de-intercalation and adsorption/desorption. Therefore, the capacitance of the composites displays the great improvement. As we know alkali cations are not only adsorption/desorption at the surface sites of MnO2, but also insertion/extraction into from bulk MnO2. Therefore, the aerogel structure can efficiently improve the electrochemical performance. In addition, it is found that the capacity of the composite aerogel with the CQDs exhibit the further enhancement, which is attributed to the decreased internal resistance and the improved electrical conductivity of the MnO2/CQDs/GA. The outstanding reversibility and stability of the MnO2/CQDs/GA can be clearly observed in Fig. 6(c and d). The CV and GCD curves of the MnO2/CQDs/GA exhibit symmetrical rectangular and triangle shapes, which demonstrate that the reversible redox reaction and adsorption/desorption can occur during the charge/discharge process. Even when the scan rate increases from 5 to 100 and the current density rises from 1 to 20 A g−1, respectively, the CV and GCD curves of the MnO2/CQDs/GA still retain the rectangular and triangle shapes, indicating the preeminent rate capability of the composite aerogel. It can also be directly observed that the rate capability of the composite aerogel containing the CQDs is much superior to that of MnO2/GA and MnO2/G in Fig. 6(e). The specific capacities of the MnO2/CQDs/GA can reach 721, 700, 678, 653, 646, 643 F g−1 when the current densities are 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20 A g−1, respectively, which is significantly higher than the specific capacities of the MnO2/GA and MnO2/G at the same current densities. Besides, in comparison to capacity retentions of the MnO2/GA (66.4%) and MnO2/G (42.4%) at 20 A g−1, the MnO2/CQDs/GA displays the superior capacity retention of 89.2%. As shown in Fig. 6(f), the cycling stability was investigated by measuring 10,000 charge/discharge cycles at 10 A g−1. The MnO2/CQDs/GA shows the excellent stability due to the 92.3% capacitance retention of the initial capacity value. Simultaneously, the capacitance retention of the MnO2/GA and MnO2/G can only achieve 70.3% and 42.9%, indicating that the CQDs play an important role in the stability of the composite aerogel. The Coulombic efficiency of the three samples also shows that the synthesized samples have the excellent reversible performance. It can also be observed the slight capacity loss of the MnO2/CQDs/GA by comparing the GCD curves of the first and last cycle in Fig. S5 (a). By contrasting the GCD curves of the last cycle, the MnO2/CQDs/GA still exhibits the highest specific capacity in Fig. S5 (b). The CQDs that provide the stable links between the MnO2 nanoparticles and the graphene efficiently improve the capacity and stability of the composite aerogel from the above characterizations. The FESEM images that show the morphology of the three samples after 10,000 charge/discharge cycles in Fig. S6 further describe the effect of the CQDs in the stability improvement. As shown in Fig. S6 (a), the MnO2 nanoparticles show agglomeration to some extent, but there still remain numerous nanoparticles on the graphene aerogel. For comparison, the serious aggregation of the MnO2 nanoparticles occurs on the graphene and the number of the observed MnO2 nanoparticles has significantly decreased in Fig. S6 (b, c). The CQDs as rivet fix the MnO2 nanoparticles on the graphene nanosheets firmly, which can prevent the MnO2 nanoparticles falling away from the graphene during the charge/discharge process. The electrochemical properties of the synthesized MnO2/CQDs/GA in our work are superior to the results in current reports (Tab. S1).

To study the practical application of the obtained sample, the symmetrical supercapacitor was prepared utilizing the MnO2/CQDs/GA as the electrodes and 1 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte, which was characterized in the two-electrode system. Since the CV curves of MnO2/CQDs/GA as electrode material exhibited near-rectangle at 10 mV s−1 in the potential range from −1 to 0 V in Fig. S7, the stabilities of the symmetrical supercapacitor were explored within different voltage windows. The CV curves still maintained the rectangular shape when the voltage window increased from 1 to 2 V in Fig. S8, which could be for two aspects [57–61]. Firstly, half the mass of the synthesized MnO2/CQDs/GA are graphene which can stably provide the double layer capacitance in the large potential widow. Secondly, it was reported that the whole charge/discharge process of MnO2 may mainly involve: (i) cation transport in the electrolyte, (ii) adsorption/desorption of cations at the surface sites of electrodes ((MnO2)surface + Na+(H+) + e− ↔ (MnOONa(H))surface), (iii) cations insertion/extraction into/from bulk MnO2 ((MnO2)bulk + Na+(H+) + e− ↔ (MnOONa(H))bulk). It indicated that the MnO2 of the MnO2/CQDs/GA could not only provide the psedocapacitance but also have the double layer capacitance. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 7 (a), the near-rectangle CV curves of the symmetrical supercapacitor show the ideal reversibility and stability when the sweep rate increases from 5 to 200 mV s−1. The specific capacities of the fabricated device are estimated by the GCD curves at different current densities. As shown in Fig. 7(b), according to eq (2), the specific capacity can reach 68.76 F g-1 at 1 A g−1, which reserves 33.57 F g-1 at 20 A g−1. The 10,000 charge/discharge cycles at 20 A g−1 of the symmetrical supercapacitor were carried out to further illustrate the cycling stability. The capacity retention can achieve 92.5% after long-term cycles as shown in Fig. 7(c), which indicated that the device shows the outstanding stability. The energy and power densities are the important characterizations for practical application of the device. According to eqs (3) and (4), the energy and power densities are calculated, and displayed by the Ragone plots in Fig. 7(d). The symmetrical supercapacitor shows the high energy density of 38.2 W h kg−1 corresponding to the power density of 1 k W kg−1; The energy density of the device can still reach 18.65 W h kg−1 when the power density rises to 20 k W kg−1. The energy and power densities of the fabricated device in our work are obviously superior to the MnO2/graphene based supercapacitors in current reports [62–66]. It is illustrated that the MnO2/CQDs/GA//MnO2/CQDs/GA symmetrical supercapacitor has a bright prospect for practical application.

In summary, the composite aerogel that the CQDs anchor the MnO2 nanoparticles on the graphene was successfully synthesized, and served as the electrode materials for the symmetrical supercapacitor. By contrasting the MnO2/GA and MnO2/G, the MnO2/CQDs/GA exhibits the excellent electrochemical performance, which attribute to the formation of three-dimensional network structure and the connection function of the CQDs. The 3D network structure can avoid the reunion of the graphene nanosheets and the MnO2 nanoparticles, which enlarge the areas of the electrode material contacting with the electrolyte, increase the active sites of the electrode material, form the interlacing conductive network, and reduce the diffusion path. Moreover, the CQDs reinforce the connection between the MnO2 nanoparticles and the graphene, which improve the stability and electrical conductivity effectively. The high-performance symmetrical supercapacitor device was fabricated with the synthesized MnO2/CQDs/GA electrodes. The device displays the high energy density of 38.2 W h kg−1 corresponding to the power density of 1 k W kg−1, and the energy density can still retain 18.65 W h kg−1 when the power density is achieved 20 k W kg−1. Therefore, the advantages of the MnO2/CQDs/graphene composite aerogel as an electrode material show the bright prospects for the application in the high-performance supercapacitors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 51102010, 21336003, 21371021), the National Key Research Program of China (No. 2016YFB0901500) and Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No: 14DZ2261000).
